speaker: interviewer
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you sit down and make yourself comfortable. Relax.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got bars, right now. And we're rolling, and anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is January 27, 1995. The survivor interviewed is Regina Lewin. The interviewer is Deborah [? Kattler-Kupetz ?] in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Regina Lewin. I was born Szwarcfeld. I was born in Bedzin, Poland. It's a little town, predominantly Jewish, and it's not far from the German border. It sits mostly on coal mines. I grew up in a city where there was a lot of smoke, and we had coal for all the time. We kept warm. At least we had warm, because we lived above coal mines. My mother's name was [? Sala. ?] Her maiden name was Goldfinger. My father's name was Hersz Szwarcfeld, 'Schwartzfeld,' if it's easier. I was an only child. I was born on October 3, 1927, in Bedzin, and the next large city was [? Katzevich, ?] if it helps. I went to public school. This city was divided by choice. The Jewish community, the Jewish population, had a female-- a girl-- Jewish school. It was supported by the Jews, but it wasn't a private school. It was public, but all girls-- all girls, all Jewish girls. There were two or three schools like that. There were boys schools, Jewish boys school, and Jewish girls school, which meant that Saturday and Sunday, we didn't go to school. Sunday was normal, because Poland was predominantly Catholic, but the Jewish community tried to keep Saturday off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Monday to Friday, we went to school. I do remember very heavy winter time. I'm also remember my mother. She was very good to me. She used to walk with me to school. And in those days, the children were very respectful towards the teachers. And we liked it. We had a nice life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every summer, my mother used to send me out of this smoggy town, because it was crowded, the air wasn't too good. And my grandparents used to live in the country, in very small towns, villages. These were what they call, now, in America, [? the shtetl, ?] very little towns. I do remember, as a child, some of the towns had no electricity. They used to use kerosene lamps, and it was strange, because there weren't big buildings. I remember a childhood with very little modern life, because every summer, I used to go to my grandmother's place. I mention grandmother, because my mother's father died before I was born. I don't remember my other grandfather. My father had parents. My father had a mother and a father, but, for some reason, I used to visit more my mother's mother. And then I met my other grandmother when my grandfather, my paternal grandfather, died. Grandma went to her daughter's house. She lived with her daughter, which was a very close little village, adjacent to the one where my mother's mother lived, so I used to spend my summer vacation with the other aunt. This was my aunt. She was very good to me. I was, sometimes-- I wasn't such a good eater. And I think back, now, how I hated to go to these family people. They used to feed me. It's hard to believe, because now I'm overweight, and I look fat. I was a very skinny child, and I was fussy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to beg my parents every June, when school closed in Poland-- do I have to go away, do I have to go? What really bothered me also is that none of my parents joined me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to pick me up at the end of the summer, but they put me on a bus, which was an autobus. It was a transportation which went from Bedzin to this town, which was called [? Zarki. ?] A Polish name, it's with a Z, [? Zarki. ?] It was a little town with lots of trees and forests, and very nice. I used to make friends. They used to look forward to meet me every summer. But I just, I was feeling that they make me eat, they make me go to bed too much, or rest too much. They really pampered me. They thought that maybe they'll fatten me up, when I get home. And my aunt was very nice. She used to buy me clothes. Life in Poland wasn't, especially in our house, wasn't that wealthy. And my father used to work, used to do the upper part of the shoe. I really don't know if there is such a trade in America. You didn't used to make the sole, just the soft part of the shoe. It was like a custom-made shoe, so one would do this part, and the rest would be done by a shoemaker. Well, it's partly shoemaker work. My mother never worked. I don't remember her working. As a matter of fact, somehow-- I don't understand it now-- it was like a status that, when you're married, you have to be a housewife, you have to take care of your family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going to work was almost an insult. What is it, your husband doesn't make enough money, you have to go and work or help him? Unless they had a business, because, I remember, I had aunts and uncles that owned groceries, and had all kinds of private-- like, their own. They owned their own business, so the wife also went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But most of the time, my mother was home, when I used to come home. They did observe religion. There were-- Sabbath was very strict. Most of the people in Bedzin predominantly were orthodox. I don't recall anyone that I remember not being orthodox. The stores were closed Friday afternoon, and the Sabbath was observed very much. My father was an observant person. He used to pray. He used to put his prayer shawl every morning. I remember him praying every morning. And then-- but my mother never covered her-- she wasn't wearing a wig. She was wearing, more or less, modern clothes, like in those days-- mostly basic clothes, but not very religious, like the people that are very orthodox. They were modern orthodox, to the extent that my father would not allow me to belong to any organization, any movement, or any Zionist organization, because I thought, it's against religion. I don't know. He was kind of strict about it. He wanted me to learn how to pray and how to go to temple-- little temples, it was in Poland-- well, only during the war, when-- which I'll go back later to it. I used to enjoy playing with children in our building. I'll mention this place, because it was a very big building. Most of the people from my hometown know it, because it had three entrances. It was a very big corner building. And we had three movie theatres in this town, only three. Just modern times-- but this was, that's all we had. And one of the movie theatres was in this building. That's why people know where I lived. After the Second World War, when we went back to visit, everybody was surprised. That movie theatre still exists, and it has the same name as it used to have then. [NON-ENGLISH], which is something to do with news, with new something. So most of the people knew where I lived, where I grew up, because we had the movie theatre. And in the very corner, there was a beautiful restaurant, which was very unusual for this town-- modern, very elegant. And rich people used to-- I used to like to stand outside and watch the rich people, all dressed up with gloves and hats, go into this restaurant. Because the average people didn't go to restaurants, like in America. Only the very wealthy ones. I think that the standard of living was divided into rich and poor. There was very little middle class where I grew up. That's what I remember, as a child. And most of the time on the holidays, like Passover, my father would invite some of the children from our floor, from the place where I lived, because I was the only child. And to him, it was a good deed. It was great to invite if-- a next-door neighbor, or down the hall, somebody had five, six children or more. And he knew how hard food and the cost of living was, so he would ask my mother if it's OK to have somebody to celebrate Passover, or a high holy day, or important holiday with us. And so that's how I kept a close relationship with one of the boys, which I consider him like my brother. All the years from my childhood, I felt that he's like my brother. He had brothers and sisters, but he used to like to spend a lot of time with me, until we were separated during the war, and I never found him anymore, later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldberg. [? Yehuda ?] Goldberg. We used to call him, for short, [? Idek. ?] It's like Ike, in English, but I used to call him Idek. I still see his picture in my face. He was, from childhood, he used to come in-- to the extent that his mother used to say, I'm wondering, is he my son or yours, because he was always in our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother was a very good person. She would feed people. She would ask, because I still don't know-- this is one thing that I will never find out, because nobody's around to tell me-- why was I an only child? I don't know. Was it the economy? Possible. It's possible. She never worked. We weren't very wealthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the other hand, there was no way to prevent pregnancies or having children, so-- and being that my father was religious, I don't know. I asked, after the war, I found one aunt, in Belgium, to which I'll talk to you later about. She moved out from Poland so long ago that-- and, in those days, they didn't call each other, or maybe they didn't write about their personal life. I never know. Until the age of 12, I was an only child, and I don't know why. I assume it was the economy. I assumed that she didn't feel that-- but, and again, all the other people had so many kids, around us, down the hall. And like I said, my father invited children to eat with us-- only on special occasions, not during the week, but Sabbath or on a holiday. I'm just thinking what else to-- from school, I was an average student. I think I was doing pretty well. I was pretty bright, because my parents always used to say, you concentrate on education, because during the war, when I really needed to know chores and stuff to survive, I was very clumsy, kind of, because my mother would spoil me. I was the only one, and she would say, you read a book, you don't have to do things in the house, which was really hard, later on, because I was forced to do things. Now, one very important moment, if I can go to 1939 already, OK, because summer of 1939, as every summer, I went to my grandma's house. Actually, my aunt, because my grandma lived with a daughter. Especially in those days-- which I still think back, it's beautiful-- no parents were put in an old age home, no par-- I don't remember as a child that there were-- there weren't any old age homes, in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think, if one parent died, the oldest daughter, or whoever could afford it financially, would keep the mother or the father in her place. And this aunt kept her mom, which was my father's mother, my grandma.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have pictures from-- with my-- from this holiday, from this last holiday. My grandma was very nice. She was very old-fashioned, and she used to tell me stories from way before First World War, and stuff like that. I didn't know that something like that was going to happen, later on. And in that summer, the Nazis marched into Poland, and I was stuck. I remained with my family, and I didn't know if my parents are alive. It was September, 1939. I'm ready to go back to Bedzin to start school, because September was the beginning of a new school year, and this was my last year of public school. I finished sixth grade, and I was entering the seventh grade. And as a rule, either my mother or my father, or both parents, used to take that autobus, that transportation, and come to visit grandma, visit the family, and take me home. I couldn't go, because there was no transportation. There was such a situation. There was a bomb. They threw a bomb on this little town, this little village, because all the windows were shuttered, and there was no electricity, and we heard screaming and noises that night. And when the daylight came, the city was occupied by German soldiers. That's all I knew. There was a lot of screaming, and there was shooting. There was such a noise, like an earthquake. The windows, everything was shaking, because nearby, somewhere, they threw a bomb.

speaker: interviewer
text: I still don't know why, because nobody told me there's a war going on and start. And why would they march in? They were getting closer to occupy Poland. So my question was, what happened to my hometown? Where are my parents? How am I going to go back? Well, can you imagine, you're almost, you're 12 years old, you're lost? My family, my aunt and my grandmother, wanted me to go back to my parents. They thought, whatever happens to them, they shouldn't feel responsible if I'm stuck. But I couldn't do anything about it. So we waited a few days, until everything settled down. As a matter of fact, my grandma and my aunt, they had a big house, which had a big garden, like a big back yard. And when we woke up in the morning, or when we looked out, there were horses. They were digging out everything that grew. There were horses that the German soldiers put in that, in that yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] It was a big house. She grew a lot of stuff there. And we were occupied by military, not knowing what happened. Then we found out that they took the prominent Jews, the important Jews from the town as hostages.

speaker: interviewer
text: They apparently never returned, because they took them as hostages, lined them up, and I think that they were shot at that time, because I, I couldn't find out too much, and all I wanted to know, what is going to happen? Are my parents alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know until I came back, which was-- it took, maybe, a week. Then the city settled down, and the Polish government and the mayor of the town tried to quieten down the residents. So I went myself, a child. I grew up overnight. I don't remember, now, thinking, can a 12-year-old-- I went, myself, to the mayor of this little town, and I told him, I'm not a resident from here. I just came for my summer vacation, like I always come. I told him who my family is, and I would like to make it back to my hometown. See, I don't even know if my parents are alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he promised that he'll write down, like, a permission. But he told me, the trains are derailed. Everything is out of order. There is no transportation. How in the world will you go? So my aunt, she knew the city well, so she got in touch with a guy, like, a horse and carriage guy, but it wasn't just a carriage, it was like a wagon with horses. And, right away, people were trying to make business, to do something. So he was, like, the next best transportation. So apparently, he gathered some other people that were vacationing in this town, so he put us on that wagon. It was like a wagon, and it was hours of driving, with horses. It was quite a few hours by transportation like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my aunt said goodbye to me with tears in her eyes, and crying, and she says, I'm putting you on this wagon, and I don't even know if you have someone to, to find. But if you insist so much you do want to go, and I don't want to feel responsible. Whatever, we don't know what's going to happen to them. And unfortunately, they, they never survived, you see. So I'm on that wagon, and what my aunt did, which was amazing-- this was September, and I'll never forget-- it was close to the Jewish holidays, close to Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. And she knew, if my parents are alive, they probably won't have a live chicken, or something to-- for the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's something amazing. Children these days would never believe. She took a briefcase, a regular briefcase, and she tied the legs from the chicken. She stuck the little head out through the opening from the briefcase, so that the chicken is alive while I, if I get home, when I get home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she put some dry goods, I don't know what it was-- some, some flour-- not flour, some, I don't know, like oats, and stuff, some food that I could bring home, because she had-- they were quite wealthy. They had their own-- they grew their own stuff, and they had a lot of food, more than in big cities, because this was, like, farmers. They usually had more. They had fresh flowers and stuff. And she gave me a whole bunch of provision to take with me. And I'm on this wagon-- it's like a carriage, but it's big and very bulky-- with strangers, which I don't know who they are, and well, they disappeared, but she paid that guy, that guy that was carrying us back. And we drove and drove.

speaker: interviewer
text: We finally reached, almost, at the border of the town. My first sight was a burned-down synagogue. When I walked in, and here, I'm alone, and I see the town is half burned. So where are my parents, what am I going to do? But I'm continuing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to this guy, in Polish-- and it was probably because he wasn't Jewish, he just got paid to take us-- to get me to this street where this big building is. And he let me off there, and I'm walking up the stairs, and I found my parents. They were alive. They didn't know how to find me. They didn't know how to get me. That was the biggest welcome I could ever get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when this guy returned back to my aunt, when we drove back, he let them know that I found my parents. And this was our first holiday, the first holiday during Second World War. In 1939, when they started to get organized, what to do-- at that point, we didn't know what the future-- we didn't know that they're going to plan to destroy us. When you go back a little bit, and think back to your childhood, did you have friends that weren't Jewish? Did you have Polish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not too many. Yes. I even had one little picture, here, of a neighbor-- a little boy, a blond boy. He was a son of a hairdresser that used to live down the road, and he-- but he never went to school with me. As I mentioned, my school was Jewish, Jewish. It was called-- in Polish, it was called the female Jewish public school in this town. But I did. This little boy, he was my friend. And then there was the caretaker that lived-- most of the Jewish buildings in our town, and in all Poland, probably, had a caretaker or a handyman that used to fix things. But, sadly enough, Jewish girls were scared, scared of, of little boys, of non-Jewish boys. See, I used to have long hair. I used to have [? plait ?] tresses, long, you know, like, all the way down. They were cut off when I went to camp, shaved. And we get back later to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to pull our hair, or they used to scare us, just tease us, probably. But this little boy, he was my protector. I used to play with him. I used to see him. So he used to say to me, in Polish-- and I spoke only Polish, at home. My mother used to speak very well Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to speak more Yiddish when I met Jack. Because when I met him, I used to mix a little bit Yiddish and Polish. He is very, as he probably mentioned to you, is very interested in Yiddish culture and a lot of Jewish books.

speaker: interviewer
text: He has an awful lot of Jewish literature, and he always studies Jewish writers. So he-- and I was only-- I was so young. I was 18 going on 19 when we met. So he figured, this is a good time for me-- why don't you learn a little bit about your own language? Forget Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't forget it. I still use it. I still write it. And at home, we used to speak it, so it was fluent to me. It was very comfortable to speak Polish. I still do. When I went back, they were amazed to see that, after all these years, I can still communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939, do you remember hearing anything about war or Hitler in your home, in your parents' discussions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All that my mother was talking about is how she survived First World War. And my father, too, they used to say that they were scared, because there were cossacks, and there were Germans. And one time, one part of Poland was German, one time, until 19-- because 1918, the war was over, and I was born 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the life was all gone. We didn't talk. As a matter of fact, we talk about it more now, because we suffered so much more. We lost so much more. They did never said anything. Yes, I do remember that, when the government changed-- the Polish government changed, there was a republic or something-- they used to say, who is good to the Jews? Who, which prime minister is helping? Which prime minister in Poland will be protecting us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that was-- there was also a lot of talk that we should have our own country. This, I do remember, because the Zionist organizations were throwing a lot of propaganda. And, personally, I wish that my family, like some of the families, risked their life and went to Israel. They survived. Some of them did. Of course, it was a very hard life, because the English occupation was in Israel. There was no, no state of Israel, but people didn't-- they didn't care. I mean, we lived a peaceful life, and people, people went their own business. People would survive somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the boys sometimes teasing you or pulling your hair, did you experience any anti-Semitism that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't meet that many non-Jewish people to feel that I'm not welcome, because it was family, and the social life was different. It was, [? always, ?] we didn't throw parties. Or, you know, you mixed in the synagogue. You went to temple, and my father would take me with when they went to go do some prayers, and my mother would sit where the women sat. And so I didn't experience, I didn't even feel it. I was free. I didn't get scared to go outside. But at night, most of time, when, like, like I said, they weren't comfortable. They wouldn't-- well, a 12-year-old or 10-year-old or even less doesn't run around at night, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it all came to a shock, a big shock, because we had a curfew during the war. We didn't see anything like that. And the big streets were closed for us, the major streets in town. When you think back to your life before the war, what are some of the first memories that come to mind? [SIGH]

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish, religious life, smelling of good cooking and baking, and oh, not much, really. Like, a child-- neighbors, meeting some neighbors and going shopping with my mother. Also, I remember she used to have a sister not far from us, like, practically, well, a few kilometers, only. We, we could even walk, but we used to take a streetcar. There was a streetcar in my town, but it didn't go through the local streets. It used to go from one home, one little town. So I remember going to visit my aunt, and her two boys-- she had two boys, and I was an only girl-- so they were like my brothers. As a matter of fact, right after Second World War, that one, he's here, and he was interviewed already. He's working. He's a volunteer with the Shoah program. He met me. He found me, right after Second World War, because we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my memories of my childhood were being with family, with aunts and uncles and cousins. Nothing special. Didn't travel anywhere, except for summer vacation to grandma, you know, to the little town, which, I really, it was a miracle. I still cannot believe how I made it back home that time. And I stayed until 1942, with my parents. But it got harder and harder, and the new laws came out all the time. So, now, we already under occupation, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now is a good time to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was perfect [LAUGHTER] Yeah. That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's perfect. When you--
